explicit_text: In the fall of 1963, a group of us sent a letter to the town officials in Clinton asking for a biracial committee. We also organized picketing in Clinton to push for civil rights, which led to arrests, including juveniles being sent to jail. The town was very resistant, and even after all this, there still wasn't a biracial committee established.
implicit_text: In the fall of 1963, a group of us sent a letter to the local officials in the small Louisiana town asking for a biracial committee. We also organized picketing there to push for civil rights, which led to arrests, including juveniles being sent to jail. The town was very resistant, and even after all this, there still wasn't a biracial committee established.

explicit_text: After we sent the letter in September 1963 asking for a biracial committee, the district attorney replied with accusations that biracial committees were part of a communist plot. Shortly after, we were subpoenaed to court repeatedly, and eventually some of us were arrested on charges like public intimidation and criminal conspiracy. The district attorney played a key role in opposing our efforts.
implicit_text: After we sent the letter in September 1963 asking for a biracial committee, the chief prosecutor replied with accusations that biracial committees were part of a communist plot. Shortly after, we were subpoenaed to court repeatedly, and eventually some of us were arrested on charges like public intimidation and criminal conspiracy. The chief prosecutor played a key role in opposing our efforts.

explicit_text: The Sheriff's Office was one of the entities we addressed in our letter asking for a biracial committee in Clinton. After the letter, deputies came to arrest me and others on December 3rd, charging us with public intimidation and criminal conspiracy. They handled our booking and jailing, and while in jail, we were treated fairly and given supper.
implicit_text: The local law enforcement office was one of the entities we addressed in our letter asking for a biracial committee in that Louisiana town. After the letter, deputies came to arrest me and others on December 3rd, charging us with public intimidation and criminal conspiracy. They handled our booking and jailing, and while in jail, we were treated fairly and given supper.

explicit_text: During the court hearings after our letter, we were asked if we were members of CORE, the Congress of Racial Equality. We didn't answer those questions without counsel. CORE was part of the broader civil rights movement context in which we were operating, and our involvement was scrutinized by authorities.
implicit_text: During the court hearings after our letter, we were asked if we were members of the national civil rights organization known for nonviolent protests. We didn't answer those questions without counsel. That organization was part of the broader civil rights movement context in which we were operating, and our involvement was scrutinized by authorities.

explicit_text: Alice Thompson and I were subpoenaed by the Grand Jury around late November or early December 1963 to answer questions about the letter we sent in Clinton. Neither of us knew much about the letter's origins because we weren't in Clinton when it was written. We both refused to disclose certain information, and shortly after, arrests were made.
implicit_text: A woman named Alice Thompson and I were subpoenaed by the Grand Jury around late November or early December 1963 to answer questions about the letter we sent in that Louisiana town. Neither of us knew much about the letter's origins because we weren't there when it was written. We both refused to disclose certain information, and shortly after, arrests were made.

explicit_text: When the Grand Jury questioned me about the letter, I told them I wasn't in Clinton when it was written because I was in Plaquemine at the time. This helped me truthfully say I didn't know who wrote the letter or who made people sign it. Plaquemine was where I was staying during that summer, away from the direct events in Clinton.
implicit_text: When the Grand Jury questioned me about the letter, I told them I wasn't in the town when it was written because I was in a nearby Louisiana city at the time. This helped me truthfully say I didn't know who wrote the letter or who made people sign it. That city was where I was staying during that summer, away from the direct events in the town.

explicit_text: Ed Vickery was arrested around the same time as the rest of us who signed the letter, either a day before or after our arrests in December 1963. He was taken to the Baton Rouge jail. His arrest was part of the broader crackdown on those involved in civil rights activities in Clinton.
implicit_text: A man named Ed Vickery was arrested around the same time as the rest of us who signed the letter, either a day before or after our arrests in December 1963. He was taken to the jail in the state capital. His arrest was part of the broader crackdown on those involved in civil rights activities in the Louisiana town.

explicit_text: Ed Vickery was taken to the jail in Baton Rouge after his arrest related to the letter we sent in Clinton. Baton Rouge served as a detention location for some of the activists during that period. It was part of the regional law enforcement response to the civil rights activities.
implicit_text: Ed Vickery was taken to the jail in the Louisiana state capital after his arrest related to the letter we sent in that town. The capital city served as a detention location for some of the activists during that period. It was part of the regional law enforcement response to the civil rights activities.

explicit_text: After some of the juveniles were arrested for picketing in Clinton in October 1963, they were transferred to the West Baton Rouge Parish Jail. The juveniles spent about seven out of ten days there. This transfer was part of the authorities' efforts to manage and control the young protesters.
implicit_text: After some of the juveniles were arrested for picketing in the Louisiana town in October 1963, they were transferred to the parish jail in a neighboring parish. The juveniles spent about seven out of ten days there. This transfer was part of the authorities' efforts to manage and control the young protesters.
cues: transfer to West Baton Rouge Parish Jail

explicit_text: Three of us who were arrested during the Clinton picketing were initially held in East Feliciana Parish Jail before being transferred to New Roads. The transfer was intended to isolate us so we wouldn't influence other inmates. While in jail, we organized and successfully petitioned for better conditions.
implicit_text: Three of us who were arrested during the picketing in that town were initially held in a parish jail in East Feliciana before being transferred to another location. The transfer was intended to isolate us so we wouldn't influence other inmates. While in jail, we organized and successfully petitioned for better conditions.

explicit_text: After being arrested for picketing in Clinton, three of us were transferred from East Feliciana Parish Jail to New Roads. The authorities thought transferring us would prevent us from organizing other inmates, but we continued to push for better jail conditions successfully. New Roads became part of our civil rights struggle experience.
implicit_text: After being arrested for picketing in the Louisiana town, three of us were transferred from the East Feliciana Parish Jail to a facility in New Roads. The authorities thought transferring us would prevent us from organizing other inmates, but we continued to push for better jail conditions successfully. That town became part of our civil rights struggle experience.
cues: transfer from East Feliciana Parish Jail

explicit_text: I was involved in organizing the picketing in Clinton after the voter registration campaign in the summer of 1963. We felt the boycott and picketing would help people realize the importance of voting. I was also arrested during the picketing and charged with criminal conspiracy, and I took pictures documenting the events.
implicit_text: I was involved in organizing the picketing in that Louisiana town after the voter registration campaign in the summer of 1963. We felt the boycott and picketing would help people realize the importance of voting. I was also arrested during the picketing and charged with criminal conspiracy, and I took pictures documenting the events.

explicit_text: Henry Brown was one of the young men from West Feliciana Parish involved in a demonstration in March 1964. He and four others decided to take action, showing the ongoing activism in the region beyond Clinton. Henry's participation highlights the broader involvement of people from nearby parishes in the civil rights movement.

It shows how the movement extended beyond Clinton and Baton Rouge.

explicit_text: I was fired from my job as a library assistant on the Negro bookmobile after being subpoenaed several times in connection with civil rights activities. The library was segregated, with separate bookmobiles and cards for Negroes and whites. The CORE library sit-in was part of the broader struggle to desegregate public facilities, and my firing was a direct result of my activism.
implicit_text: I was fired from my job as a library assistant on the segregated bookmobile serving African Americans after being subpoenaed several times in connection with civil rights activities. The library was segregated, with separate bookmobiles and cards for Black and white patrons. The sit-in at the library was part of the broader struggle to desegregate public facilities, and my firing was a direct result of my activism.
